did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Machine Learning (Support Vector Machine with GLCM features) and Deep Learning (Convolutional Neural Network) to perform multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models for multi-class skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methodology including preprocessing (Dull Razor, SMOTE-ENN), feature extraction (GLCM), and model architecture, and reports new empirical results (98% accuracy for SVM, 99.30% for CNN). Although published in a conference proceedings (IC-CGU), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Machine Learning Approaches for Multi-class Classification of Skin Cancer
journal: 2024 1st International Conference on Cognitive, Green and Ubiquitous Computing (IC-CGU)
brief_summary: This study compares Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. To address class imbalance, the SVM approach utilized data augmentation, while the CNN approach employed the SMOTE-ENN hybrid sampling technique. The SVM model achieved an overall testing accuracy of 98%, whereas the CNN model achieved a higher accuracy of 99.30%. The results indicate that the CNN architecture, supported by effective preprocessing and sampling, outperformed the classical SVM method in classifying the seven skin cancer classes.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin cancer lesions (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study compares Support Vector Machine (SVM) with GLCM features and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to predict discrete diagnostic categories. The CNN uses a softmax output layer for 7 classes. Reported metrics include accuracy (98% for SVM, 99.30% for CNN), AUC-ROC, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The dataset consists of 10015 RGB images (size 600x450) belonging to seven different classes of skin cancer. More than 50% of the images are confirmed through histopathology, while others are consensus-approved or confirmed through follow-up examination. Metadata including image ID, class, sex, localization, and confirmed resource is provided in a CSV file.

use.training: Used for training both the SVM model (after augmentation to 13309 images) and the CNN model (after SMOTE-ENN sampling to 43473 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during CNN training (monitoring validation loss for early stopping).
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate final model performance (SVM achieved 98% accuracy, CNN achieved 99.30% accuracy).

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the classification performance of the SVM and CNN models across different skin cancer classes.
To prevent overfitting by monitoring validation loss and stopping training when no improvement occurred for over 5 epochs.
To optimize model convergence, with a minimum target learning rate of 0.0001.

statistical_methods_used.method: SMOTE-ENN (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique combined with Edited Nearest Neighbors)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance in the dataset for the CNN model by oversampling minority classes and removing noise/irrelevant data points.
To increase the number of training images for the SVM model to handle class imbalance.
To provide class-wise performance evaluation and compare the SVM and CNN models, particularly highlighting performance on imbalanced classes like Dermatofibroma.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC-ROC, precision, recall, and F1-score without statistical significance testing.

SVM_with_GLCM.notes: The SVM model used GLCM features extracted from augmented images (total 13309). The AUC-ROC curve showed the highest score for Vascular lesion and lowest for Dermatofibroma. Performance was depreciated due to lack of training in certain classes.

CNN_with_SMOTE_ENN.notes: The CNN model used SMOTE-ENN for balancing (total 43473 images). Early stopping was applied with a minimum learning rate of 0.0001. The model achieved perfect AUC scores for all classes.

CNN_with_Imbalanced_Data.notes: Results obtained for the CNN architecture with the original imbalanced dataset (10015 images) to demonstrate the impact of class imbalance.

training_details.loss_function: Categorical Crossentropy (implied by softmax and multi-class classification, though not explicitly named, 'accuracy' was the metric)
training_details.early_stopping: True (patience: 5 epochs, monitored on validation loss)

preprocessing_techniques: Dull Razor algorithm for hair removal
Data augmentation (horizontal flip, Gaussian noise, contrast adjustment) for SVM pipeline
SMOTE-ENN (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique combined with Edited Nearest Neighbors) for CNN pipeline
Image resizing (to 64x64 for SVM/GLCM pipeline; to 28x28 for CNN pipeline)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to minority class images in the SVM pipeline to balance the dataset.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: SMOTE-ENN (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique combined with Edited Nearest Neighbors)
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for the CNN pipeline to oversample minority classes and remove noise/irrelevant data points by deleting data points whose class does not match with the class of its majority nearest neighbor.

conclusions: The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture with SMOTE-ENN preprocessing outperformed the Support Vector Machine (SVM) with GLCM features, achieving a testing accuracy of 99.30% compared to 98%.
The SVM model's lower accuracy (78% on imbalanced data) was attributed to insufficient training for minority classes, particularly Dermatofibroma, which had the fewest original images (115).
The CNN model achieved near-perfect classification performance (AUC of 1.0) for all seven skin cancer classes after applying the SMOTE-ENN technique to balance the dataset.
Data augmentation (for SVM) and SMOTE-ENN sampling (for CNN) were effective strategies for handling the class imbalance inherent in the HAM10000 dataset.

The CNN architecture, optimized with early stopping and learning rate tuning, demonstrated high efficiency and robustness against overfitting, as evidenced by the small gap between training (99.98%) and testing (99.30%) accuracy.
The study confirms that machine learning and deep learning techniques can effectively assist in the early and accurate multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions.
important_remarks: The CNN model achieved near-perfect metrics (AUC of 1.0, F1-score of 1.0 for most classes) after applying SMOTE-ENN, which generated a massive synthetic training set (43,473 images) from the original 10,015, raising concerns about data leakage or over-optimistic performance estimates due to synthetic sample generation.

The study relies entirely on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., only 115 original images for Dermatofibroma) and multi-source bias; the paper does not report external validation on independent datasets.

The SVM model's performance was significantly degraded on imbalanced data (78% testing accuracy) and struggled with the minority Dermatofibroma class (F1-score 0.85, AUC 0.71), highlighting the limitation of classical ML with GLCM features on highly imbalanced clinical data without extensive synthetic sampling.

The CNN architecture uses a very small input resolution (28x28 pixels), which may discard fine-grained dermatological features critical for accurate skin cancer diagnosis compared to standard higher-resolution inputs.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance difference between SVM (98%) and CNN (99.30%).

The paper does not report results on the original imbalanced CNN model's testing accuracy, only training/testing accuracy (93%/78%), making it difficult to assess the CNN's robustness without the SMOTE-ENN intervention in a real-world clinical setting where synthetic data is not available.

The dataset contains images captured with 'less appearance of surrounding skin,' which may limit the model's ability to learn contextual cues and affects generalizability to clinical images with varying backgrounds.
